programs.name: Med-QUEST (Medicaid for seniors/disabled)
programs.tagline: Free health coverage including nursing home care and home support services for Hawaii seniors and disabled adults with limited income

localEntities.name: Contact Med-QUEST Division for specific regional eligibility offices

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ or disabled and has limited income, Med-QUEST provides comprehensive health coverage including nursing home care, home modifications, and community support services to help them stay safely at home. Because this is Hawaii's Medicaid program for seniors and disabled adults, it covers services that Medicare doesn't — like long-term care and non-medical home supports.

The program has different categories with different rules. For nursing home care, your parent must contribute nearly all their monthly income toward care costs but keeps coverage. For community-based care, income limits are around $24,826/year for a single person, with asset limits of $2,000. You must also demonstrate that your parent needs nursing home level of care or daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.

summary: Need nursing home level care or daily ADL help
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home level of care or require daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management
otherRequirements: Be a Hawaii resident
Be a U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at My Medical Benefits or call 1-877-628-5076 — processing time varies by complexity of case.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect birth certificate, Social Security card, income documentation, and bank statements before starting your application.

steps.title: Complete the Med-QUEST application
steps.description: Apply online at mybenefits.hawaii.gov or call 1-877-628-5076. If your parent is 65+, disabled, or receives SSI, you'll also need form 1100B.

steps.title: Submit functional assessment
steps.description: For long-term care services, you'll need to demonstrate nursing home level of care or ADL assistance needs through clinical evaluation.

steps.title: Wait for eligibility determination
steps.description: Med-QUEST Division will review your application and may request additional documentation or schedule assessments.

applicationGuide.tip: Even if you think your parent's income or assets are too high, apply anyway — the program has exceptions and different pathways that may still qualify them.

The 1100B form is specifically required for those 65+, disabled, blind, or receiving SSI/Social Security — don't skip this step

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for Med-QUEST long-term care?
faqs.answer: Asset limits for Med-QUEST focus on financial assets like bank accounts and investments. The home exemption rules aren't specified in available information, so call 1-877-628-5076 to get specific guidance about real estate and your parent's situation.

faqs.question: What's the difference between regular Med-QUEST and nursing home Med-QUEST?
faqs.answer: Regular Med-QUEST has income limits around 138% of Federal Poverty Level ($24,826 for singles). Nursing home Med-QUEST requires your parent to contribute nearly all their monthly income toward care costs but provides comprehensive long-term care coverage. Both require demonstrating medical need.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my parent if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of aging parents who cannot manage the process themselves. You'll need to provide all their documentation and may need to become their authorized representative through the application process.

faqs.question: Will Med-QUEST cover care at home or just nursing homes?
faqs.answer: Med-QUEST covers both nursing home care and community-based services including home modifications, non-medical support services, and assisted living residences. The goal is helping seniors stay safely at home when possible.

faqs.question: What if my parent receives Social Security — does that count as income?
faqs.answer: Yes, Social Security counts as income toward the limits. For nursing home care, your parent would contribute nearly all their Social Security toward care costs. For community-based care, Social Security counts toward the income threshold of approximately $24,826/year for singles.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both Medicare and Med-QUEST?
faqs.answer: Yes, many seniors have both Medicare and Med-QUEST (called dual eligibles). Med-QUEST can help pay Medicare premiums and cover services Medicare doesn't, like long-term care and home modifications. Don't apply through Healthcare.gov if your parent has Medicare — use mybenefits.hawaii.gov instead.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get approved and start receiving services?
faqs.answer: Processing time varies depending on your case complexity and whether clinical assessments are needed to determine nursing home level of care. Contact 1-877-628-5076 for current processing times and to check your application status.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's needs change after they're enrolled?
faqs.answer: Med-QUEST reviews cases periodically and when circumstances change. If your parent's care needs increase or decrease, contact the Med-QUEST Division to report changes and potentially adjust their services or coverage category.

programs.name: Kūpuna Care Program
programs.tagline: If your parent is 60+ in Hawaii and needs daily care help but doesn't qualify for Medicaid, this state program provides attendant care, meals, and transportation.

localEntities.name: Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC) - statewide coordination

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and needs help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or getting to appointments, but earns too much to qualify for Medicaid, Hawaii's Kūpuna Care Program can provide attendant care, transportation, home-delivered meals, and case management services. The program serves as a safety net for older adults who fall into the gap between Medicaid eligibility and being able to afford private care.

Unlike federal programs with strict income cutoffs, Kūpuna Care uses sliding-scale cost-sharing based on your parent's ability to pay. Services are provided through contracted agencies, not cash payments. The program requires an in-home assessment to determine functional needs and cannot serve anyone already receiving Medicaid or comparable government long-term care services.

Priority goes to those at greatest risk of nursing home placement, with low income, or living in rural areas. Some services may have waitlists depending on county and demand.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must have documented limitations in Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring) or Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (meal preparation, housekeeping, medication management, transportation) confirmed through in-home assessment
otherRequirements: Hawaii resident
U.S. citizen or legal alien
Cannot be receiving Medicaid or comparable government/private home and community-based services
Cannot live in nursing facility, intermediate care facility, or adult residential care home

applicationGuide.summary: Call Hawaii's Aging and Disability Resource Center at (808) 643-2372 to start the application and schedule an in-home assessment.

steps.description: Your county Area Agency on Aging will conduct initial screening and determine if your parent meets basic eligibility requirements.

steps.title: Schedule in-home assessment
steps.description: A trained assessor will visit your parent's home to evaluate functional needs, safety concerns, and care requirements. This assessment determines eligibility and service levels.

steps.title: Receive individualized support plan
steps.description: If approved, you'll receive a written plan detailing authorized services, provider contacts, and cost-sharing requirements based on income.

steps.title: Begin service coordination
steps.description: Case manager will help connect with contracted service providers and coordinate care delivery.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies by county and assessment scheduling availability
applicationGuide.waitlist: Some services may have waitlists. Priority given to those with greatest economic or social need and risk of institutional placement
applicationGuide.tip: Have employment information ready if you're a working caregiver — there's a separate Kūpuna Caregivers Program for employed family members working 30+ hours per week.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they have some assets but don't qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is exactly who Kūpuna Care serves. The program fills the gap for older adults who have too much income or assets to qualify for Medicaid but can't afford private long-term care. You'll pay based on a sliding scale according to ability to pay.

faqs.question: What if my parent already receives some Medicare home health services?
faqs.answer: Medicare home health is generally allowed, but your parent cannot receive comparable government or private home and community-based care services. The assessment team will review all current services to determine if there's any conflict.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Waitlist length varies by county, specific services needed, and priority level. Those at greatest risk of nursing home placement, with lowest income, or in rural areas get priority. Contact your county's Area Agency on Aging for current wait times.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can initiate the application process, but your parent must participate in the in-home assessment unless you have legal guardianship or power of attorney for healthcare decisions.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's needs increase after enrollment?
faqs.answer: The program includes ongoing case management and can adjust services based on changing needs. Contact your assigned case manager to request a reassessment if your parent's functional abilities decline.

faqs.question: Can my parent receive both Kūpuna Care and federal Older Americans Act services?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, since OAA Title III services (like senior centers and information services) are different from the hands-on care services Kūpuna Care provides. The assessment will determine what combination of services is appropriate.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in an assisted living facility?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot live in an adult residential care home, intermediate care facility, or skilled nursing facility to be eligible. However, independent living arrangements or senior housing may be acceptable depending on the level of care provided by the facility.

faqs.question: Is there a difference between Kūpuna Care and the Kūpuna Caregivers Program?
faqs.answer: Yes, they're separate programs. Kūpuna Care provides services directly to your parent. The Kūpuna Caregivers Program provides up to $70/day in services to support employed family caregivers working 30+ hours per week. You may be able to access both.

programs.name: Sage PLUS Program (PACE equivalent)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one Medicare counseling to help navigate benefits and coverage decisions for your Hawaii parent.

localEntities.name: Hawaii Department of Health, Executive Office on Aging

programs.intro:
If your parent has Medicare and lives in Hawaii, Sage PLUS provides free, unbiased counseling to help navigate Medicare benefits, supplement options, and health insurance decisions. This is not comprehensive medical care like traditional PACE programs — instead, trained volunteers offer one-on-one guidance on Medicare enrollment, plan comparisons, and benefit questions.

Because Medicare rules change annually and Hawaii has unique plan options, having an objective counselor can prevent costly mistakes when choosing coverage. The program serves Medicare-eligible individuals, their families, and caregivers statewide through the Hawaii State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP).

Your parent must have Medicare Part A and/or Part B and live in Hawaii to qualify. There are no income or asset limits for the counseling service itself, though some Medicare programs they help with may have financial requirements.

summary: Must have Medicare Part A or B
otherRequirements: Must have Medicare Part A and/or Part B
Must live in Hawaii
Cannot live outside the U.S.
Must actively enroll (no automatic enrollment)

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Hawaii SHIP online or by phone — no formal application required for counseling services.

steps.title: Attend counseling session
steps.description: The counselor will provide objective guidance on Medicare options, help compare plans, and explain benefits — no sales pressure or enrollment required.

applicationGuide.tip: Service availability may vary since it's volunteer-based — contact Hawaii SHIP as early as possible, especially during Medicare open enrollment periods.

faqs.q: Can I get help choosing between different Medicare Advantage plans in Hawaii?
faqs.a: Yes, Sage PLUS counselors can explain the differences between available Medicare Advantage plans in Hawaii, compare costs and benefits, and help you understand network restrictions. They cannot recommend specific plans but will provide objective information to help you decide.

faqs.q: What if my parent needs actual medical care, not just Medicare counseling?
faqs.a: Sage PLUS only provides counseling and education about Medicare benefits — it's not a healthcare program. For comprehensive medical care similar to traditional PACE, you'll need to explore other Hawaii programs or work with your parent's existing healthcare providers.

faqs.q: Can a family member call on behalf of an elderly parent?
faqs.a: Yes, family members and caregivers can contact Hawaii SHIP for information and to schedule counseling sessions. However, your parent may need to be present or provide consent for discussions about their specific Medicare coverage and personal information.

faqs.q: Is there a cost for Medicare counseling through Sage PLUS?
faqs.a: No, all Medicare counseling through Hawaii SHIP is completely free. Counselors are trained volunteers who provide unbiased information without any sales pressure or enrollment fees.

faqs.q: How long does a typical counseling session last?
faqs.a: Session length varies based on your needs and questions, but most one-on-one counseling sessions last 30-60 minutes. More complex situations involving multiple Medicare programs may require longer sessions or follow-up meetings.

faqs.q: Can I get help if my parent is already enrolled in Medicare but having problems with coverage?
faqs.a: Yes, Sage PLUS counselors can help with ongoing Medicare questions, coverage problems, billing issues, and understanding benefits even after enrollment. They can also explain appeal processes and help you understand your rights under Medicare.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery assistance for seniors 60+ on fixed incomes — Hawaii residents may qualify for food aid even with higher incomes than other states.

localEntities.name: Department of Human Services Benefit, Employment & Support Services Division (BESSD) offices island-wide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling to afford groceries in Hawaii, SNAP provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card that works like a debit card at grocery stores. Hawaii has more generous income limits than many states — a single person can earn up to $2,998/month and still qualify, and there's no asset limit for most applicants.

Because Hawaii recognizes the state's high cost of living, your parent may qualify even if they wouldn't in other states. The program covers groceries, produce, and basic food items but cannot be used for prepared foods, alcohol, or tobacco. Monthly benefit amounts depend on household size and income after deductions, with special deductions available for seniors including medical expenses over $35/month.

Many eligible seniors don't apply because they assume their Social Security or pension income is too high. Hawaii's Broad-Based Categorical Eligibility means traditional federal limits don't apply — it's worth checking even if your parent's income seems borderline.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly EBT benefits based on household size and income after deductions

otherRequirements: Hawaii resident
U.S. citizen, legal permanent resident (5+ years), or qualified non-citizen
Social Security number (or application pending)
Must include household members who buy and prepare food together

applicationGuide.tip: Don't forget to report medical expenses over $35/month — this deduction can significantly increase your parent's monthly benefits.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and car if they apply for SNAP?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hawaii uses Broad-Based Categorical Eligibility, which means there's no asset limit for most applicants. Your parent can own their home, vehicles, and reasonable savings without affecting SNAP eligibility. Only income matters for most households.

faqs.question: What if my parent's Social Security puts them over the income limit?
faqs.answer: Hawaii's income limit is higher than most states — $2,998/month for a single person (200% of federal poverty level). Even if gross income is higher, seniors can qualify through net income after deductions like medical expenses over $35/month, housing costs, and a 20% deduction on earned income.

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent cannot apply themselves due to age or disability. You can serve as an authorized representative by completing the appropriate forms during the application process. You'll need to provide your contact information and may be asked to attend the eligibility interview.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive in SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Benefit amounts vary based on household size and net income after deductions. Hawaii benefits are higher than mainland states due to cost of living. A rough estimate: every $100 increase in net income reduces benefits by about $30. Medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs can significantly increase the final amount.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP benefits to buy prepared foods or meals?
faqs.answer: No, SNAP benefits cannot be used for hot/prepared foods, alcohol, tobacco, vitamins, or non-food items. They can buy groceries, produce, meat, dairy, bread, cereals, and other food for home preparation. Some states have restaurant programs for elderly/disabled, but Hawaii does not currently participate.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income or medical expenses change after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Report changes within 10 days to avoid overpayments. Increased medical expenses or reduced income can increase benefits, while higher income may reduce them. Hawaii conducts periodic reviews (usually every 12 months for seniors) to adjust benefits. Call your local DHS office or the benefits helpline at (855) 643-1643 to report changes.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for SNAP and Medicaid at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply for both programs simultaneously through the same mybenefitshawaii.org portal. Many documents overlap, and applying together can save time. Both programs have similar residency and citizenship requirements, though income limits differ.

faqs.question: How long do SNAP benefits last, and when do they expire?
faqs.answer: SNAP benefits are loaded monthly onto an EBT card. Unused benefits remain on the card for 365 days from the date they were issued — after that, they expire. Benefits are typically loaded on the same date each month based on the last digit of your case number.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Help with electric and gas bills when your parent can't keep up with rising energy costs

localEntities.name: Hawaii Island: Hawaii County Economic Opportunity Council (HCEOC)

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay electric or gas bills in Hawaii, LIHEAP provides one-time payments directly to the utility company to keep their power on. This federal program helps with both emergency situations (when service is about to be shut off) and regular assistance to reduce energy costs for households earning below 150% of federal poverty guidelines.

Because LIHEAP is administered separately on each island, your parent must apply through their local Community Action Agency — HCAP on Oahu, HCEOC on Hawaii Island, or MEO on Maui County. Each island has different application windows and processing limits, with some accepting only 20 applications per month.

Your parent may automatically qualify if they already receive SSI, SNAP, or TANF benefits, regardless of their other income. The program covers one assistance payment per program year (October 1 to September 30), so families must choose between regular energy credit or emergency crisis intervention.

Responsible for electric or gas bill
US citizen or lawful permanent resident in household

Must be responsible for and provide proof of utility bill (electric or gas)

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for both Energy Credit and Crisis Intervention in the same year?
faqs.answer: No, households can only receive one type of LIHEAP assistance per program year (October 1 to September 30). Your parent must choose between regular Energy Credit or emergency Crisis Intervention, but not both.

faqs.question: What if my parent already receives SNAP or SSI — do they still need to prove income?
faqs.answer: Your parent automatically qualifies for LIHEAP if they receive SSI, SNAP, or TANF benefits, regardless of other income. However, they still need to provide proof of these benefits and complete the full application with all required documents.

faqs.question: How quickly can my parent get help if their electricity is about to be shut off?
faqs.answer: Crisis intervention cases with disconnection notices may receive priority processing, but you still need to apply through the monthly cycle system. Hawaii Island accepts only 20 applications per month, so call early in the monthly cycle for the best chance.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent who lives on Hawaii Island?
faqs.answer: Your parent must sign the application themselves, but you can help gather documents and accompany them through the process. Contact HCEOC to discuss accommodation options if your parent has difficulty with the application process due to age or disability.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent misses the June application window on Oahu?
faqs.answer: Regular Energy Credit applications are only accepted June 1-30 annually on Oahu through HCAP. If your parent misses this window, they can only apply for Crisis Intervention if they face disconnection. Otherwise, they must wait until the following June.

faqs.question: Does my parent's house value affect their LIHEAP eligibility?
faqs.answer: No, LIHEAP has no asset limits. Your parent can own their home regardless of value and still qualify based on income. The program focuses on household income below 150% of federal poverty guidelines, not assets or property ownership.

faqs.question: Can my parent get LIHEAP assistance for propane or other heating fuels?
faqs.answer: LIHEAP in Hawaii specifically covers electric and gas bills for heating and cooling assistance. Contact your island's Community Action Agency to confirm if propane or other fuel sources qualify under their local program implementation.

faqs.question: What if my parent rents and the utility bill is in the landlord's name?
faqs.answer: Your parent must be responsible for and provide proof of the utility bill to qualify for LIHEAP. If utilities are included in rent, they typically won't be eligible unless they can show separate billing responsibility for electric or gas service.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements for Hawaii families earning up to $31,200-$102,720 depending on household size.

localEntities.name: Hawaii County: HCEOC, 47 Rainbow Drive, Hilo[2]

programs.intro:
If your parent's income is below $31,200 (for a single person) or $42,240 (for a two-person household), they may qualify for free home energy improvements worth thousands of dollars. The Weatherization Assistance Program provides energy audits and installs improvements like solar water heaters, energy-efficient air conditioners, LED lighting, and refrigerator replacements at no cost.

Priority goes to households with adults 60+ or 65+, people with disabilities, and families with high energy bills. Because funding is limited each year, not all eligible applicants receive services — applying early in the year increases your parent's chances. The program is administered by different organizations on each island: HCAP on Oahu, HCEOC on Hawaii County, and KEO on Kauai.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they rent their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, renters can qualify, but the landlord must give written permission for the improvements. The landlord cannot raise rent for one year after improvements are completed, and the tenant gets the energy savings benefits.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the limit but they have high medical expenses?
faqs.answer: The program uses gross income without medical expense deductions. However, if your parent receives any amount of TANF, SSI, LIHEAP, or lives in HUD housing, they automatically qualify regardless of other income.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: There's no formal waitlist — it's based on annual funding limits. Applications submitted early in the federal fiscal year (October-December) have better chances. If funding runs out, you'll need to reapply the following year.

faqs.question: What improvements will my parent actually get?
faqs.answer: After approval, a technician conducts a home energy audit to determine what improvements provide the most energy savings for that specific home. Common improvements include LED lighting, energy-efficient air conditioners, solar or hybrid heat pump water heaters, and refrigerator replacement.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help with the application process. However, the eligible person (your parent) must be present during the home energy audit and sign necessary paperwork as the homeowner or authorized tenant.

faqs.question: Can my parent get both Weatherization and LIHEAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, coordination between these programs may provide additional benefits. LIHEAP recipients automatically qualify for Weatherization income-wise, and receiving both can maximize energy assistance benefits.

programs.name: Hawaii SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Free, expert Medicare counseling to help your parent navigate coverage options and find cost-saving programs.

localEntities.name: Administered by Hawaii Department of Health, Executive Office on Aging; volunteer counselors statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is on Medicare or turning 65 soon, Hawaii SHIP provides free, one-on-one counseling to help them understand their coverage options. Because Medicare has multiple parts (A, B, C, D), Medigap policies, and prescription drug plans, many families feel overwhelmed trying to choose the right combination. Hawaii SHIP trained counselors explain these options in plain English and help identify cost-saving programs your parent might qualify for.

The service covers all Medicare decisions — from initial enrollment to annual Open Enrollment changes to appeals if coverage is denied. Because it's funded by federal grants, there are no income limits or fees. Counselors also help families understand programs like Medicaid, Medicare Savings Program, and Extra Help that can reduce Medicare costs for people with limited incomes.

otherRequirements: Must be Medicare-eligible, soon-to-be eligible, or helping someone who is

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Hawaii SHIP helpline — counseling is provided immediately by phone or virtual appointment.

steps.title: Describe your needs
steps.description: Tell the counselor what Medicare questions you have — enrollment, plan comparison, appeals, or cost-saving programs.

steps.title: Get counseling
steps.description: Receive free, unbiased guidance by phone or virtual meeting. No time limits on sessions.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate — counseling provided during your call or scheduled virtual appointment
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's Medicare card and current plan documents ready when you call for the most personalized guidance.

faqs.question: Can I call on behalf of my parent who has Medicare?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hawaii SHIP counsels family caregivers who are helping aging parents navigate Medicare. You don't need written permission to get general information, though specific account details may require your parent on the call.

faqs.question: Will the counselor try to sell me insurance or favor certain plans?
faqs.answer: No. Hawaii SHIP counselors are trained volunteers who provide unbiased information. They don't sell insurance or receive commissions from any insurance company. Their job is to explain your options neutrally so you can make informed decisions.

faqs.question: Can Hawaii SHIP help if my parent's Medicare claim was denied?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hawaii SHIP counselors can walk you through the Medicare appeals process and help you understand your rights. They can explain the different levels of appeal and what documentation you'll need to challenge a denial.

faqs.question: Does this service cost anything or have income limits?
faqs.answer: Hawaii SHIP is completely free with no income or asset limits. It's funded by federal grants specifically to provide unbiased Medicare education to all beneficiaries regardless of their financial situation.

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals (via Kūpuna Aging in Place grantees)
programs.tagline: If your parent can't shop or cook safely, they may qualify for free weekly meal delivery to their home.

localEntities.name: Hawaii Meals on Wheels: P.O. Box 61194, Honolulu

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and homebound (unable to safely shop for or prepare meals), they may qualify for free weekly meal delivery through Hawaii's Kūpuna Aging in Place grantees. Because this program operates through 16 different nonprofits statewide, delivery options vary — some provide hot meals, others deliver frozen meals weekly, and coverage areas differ by island.

This isn't a single centralized program. Instead, you'll work directly with local providers like Lanakila Pacific (Oahu island-wide frozen meals), Hawaii Meals on Wheels (hot and frozen options), or Our Kupuna (Hawaii island). Some meals are free through Aging and Disability Resource Centers or health insurance, while others can be purchased with cash, check, or SNAP EBT.

Because someone must be home for deliveries (typically Monday-Friday, 8:30am-1pm), this works best for parents who have predictable schedules or caregivers who can coordinate delivery times.

faqs.question: Can I apply for multiple meal delivery services at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, but most providers coordinate to avoid duplication. Because these are separate nonprofits, you may need different services — for example, Lanakila Pacific for frozen meals and another provider for hot meal delivery. Each provider will assess your parent's specific needs and coordinate with other services they're already receiving.

faqs.question: What happens if no one is home during delivery hours?
faqs.answer: Delivery typically requires someone home Monday-Friday, 8:30am-1pm. If your parent lives alone and can't reliably be home, this program may not work. Some providers offer flexibility for medical appointments, but consistent missed deliveries usually result in service suspension. Consider coordinating with neighbors or family members for delivery times.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent qualifies for free meals versus paying?
faqs.answer: Free meals depend on qualifying through your county's Aging and Disability Resource Center or having health insurance that covers nutrition services. If your parent doesn't qualify for free meals, most providers accept cash, check, or SNAP EBT. Start by calling your county Aging office for assessment before contacting individual providers.

faqs.question: Can my parent get both hot and frozen meals?
faqs.answer: This depends on the provider and your parent's needs assessment. Hawaii Meals on Wheels offers both hot and frozen options, while Lanakila Pacific specializes in weekly frozen meal delivery. Providers typically coordinate to avoid over-serving, but different meal types may serve different needs — hot meals for daily delivery, frozen for weekly batch cooking.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives on an island without listed providers?
faqs.answer: The Kūpuna Aging in Place program operates through 16 nonprofits statewide, but not all are listed in available sources. Contact your county's Elderly Affairs division — Maui, Kauai, and other islands have their own aging offices that can connect you with local meal delivery options. Hawaii 2-1-1 can also help locate island-specific providers.

faqs.question: Does my parent need a doctor's referral for all meal delivery services?
faqs.answer: Not all providers require professional referrals, but many do. Our Kupuna specifically requires a referral letter from a social worker, case manager, or doctor. Lanakila Pacific and Hawaii Meals on Wheels may accept direct applications but still require needs assessment. If your parent has a case manager or regular doctor, start there for the referral process.

faqs.question: Can family caregivers get meal delivery if they're caring for a spouse?
faqs.answer: Yes, spouses serving as primary caregivers may qualify for meal delivery as respite support. This recognizes that caregiving can make it difficult to shop and prepare meals regularly. The needs assessment will consider both the care recipient's needs and the caregiver's situation. Younger household members with disabilities may also qualify regardless of age.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's needs change after starting meal delivery?
faqs.answer: Providers typically reassess needs periodically and can adjust service levels. If your parent's condition improves and they can shop/cook again, service may end. If needs increase, they may qualify for more frequent delivery or additional services. Contact your provider directly when your parent's situation changes rather than waiting for scheduled reassessment.

programs.name: Caregiver Support (via Executive Office on Aging)
programs.tagline: Free respite care, training, and counseling for family caregivers of adults 60+ in Hawaii.
programs.intro:
If you're caring for a parent age 60+ in Hawaii, you may qualify for free respite care, caregiver training, and emotional support through the state's Family Caregiver Support Program. Because this program operates through your local Area Agency on Aging, you'll get connected to services specifically available in your county — from support groups to temporary relief care when you need a break.

The program serves family caregivers age 18+ who are caring for someone 60 or older, with expanded eligibility for special situations like grandparents 55+ caring for children or adults caring for family members with developmental disabilities. Priority goes to caregivers with the greatest economic or social need, including minorities, rural residents, and those with limited English proficiency. You must contact your local Area Agency on Aging to apply — there's no centralized online application.

faqs.question: Can I apply for both federal and state caregiver programs at the same time?
faqs.answer: Your local Area Agency on Aging will assess which track fits your situation. Federal Title III services are free with priority given to greatest need, while state Kupuna Care requires functional assessment and income-based cost-sharing but excludes those eligible for Medicaid.

faqs.question: What if I'm 55+ caring for a grandchild or disabled adult family member?
faqs.answer: You qualify under special population rules even if your care recipient isn't 60+. This includes adults 55+ caring for non-child adults 18+ with developmental disabilities, or grandparents/relatives 55+ caring for children 18 and under.

faqs.question: How do I know which county office to call?
faqs.answer: Oahu residents call Senior Helpline (808) 768-7700. Hawaii County residents call (808) 961-8626. For other counties, check hawaiiadrc.org or call the main Executive Office on Aging for your local Area Agency contact.

faqs.question: What counts as 'greatest economic or social need' for priority?
faqs.answer: Priority goes to minorities, rural residents, those with limited English proficiency, and caregivers with significant financial constraints. Your local Area Agency will assess your specific situation during the application process.

faqs.question: Can someone apply on behalf of an elderly caregiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, a caregiver or person with power of attorney may call on behalf of the applicant. You'll need to provide the same assessment information about care needs, supports, and financial situation.

faqs.question: What's the difference between respite care and other services offered?
faqs.answer: Respite care provides temporary relief so you can take breaks from caregiving. Other services include ongoing support groups, caregiver training on specific care techniques, individual counseling, and case management to coordinate multiple services.

faqs.question: Are Native Hawaiian families eligible for additional services?
faqs.answer: Yes, Native Hawaiian caregivers of Native Hawaiian adults 60+ with chronic illness/disability have specific eligibility, as do Native Hawaiian grandparents/relatives caring for children 18 and younger. Contact your local Area Agency about culturally specific programming.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent becomes Medicaid-eligible while I'm receiving services?
faqs.answer: Federal Title III services can continue regardless of Medicaid status. However, state Kupuna Care specifically excludes Medicaid-eligible individuals, so you'd need to transition to different support services if receiving that track.

programs.name: Retired Senior Volunteer Program (RSVP, related to SCSEP)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 55+ and unemployed, they can earn Hawaii minimum wage while learning job skills through paid training placements.

localEntities.name: Hawaii County: Hilo (127 Kamana St, Rm 5, Hilo, (808)961-8730), Kona (74-5044 Ane Keohokalole Hwy, (808)323-4320)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55 or older and struggling to find work, the Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP) provides paid job training at Hawaii's minimum wage. Because the program places participants in supervised training positions at government and nonprofit organizations, your parent earns income while building skills for permanent employment.

Your parent must have family income at or below 125% of the federal poverty level and be unemployed to qualify. Priority goes to veterans and their spouses, people 65+, those with disabilities, and rural residents. Training averages 19-20 hours per week for 20-27 months, with a lifetime limit of 48 months if continuously eligible.

Because SCSEP is managed by local operators in each county, availability and waitlists vary by location. Positions are limited, so many eligible participants are placed on waiting lists until training slots open up.

Must be eligible to work in the United States

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they're receiving Social Security benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, receiving Social Security doesn't disqualify them, but the total family income (including Social Security) must be at or below 125% of the federal poverty level. Check current poverty guidelines at aspe.hhs.gov to see if your family qualifies.

faqs.question: What's the difference between RSVP and SCSEP?
faqs.answer: RSVP programs (like those in Honolulu and Hawaii County) are unpaid volunteer matching services for people 55+ with no income requirements. SCSEP is a paid training program with strict income and unemployment requirements. Make sure you're applying for the right program.

faqs.question: How long are the waitlists really?
faqs.answer: Waitlists vary by county because each area has limited training positions. Counties with higher populations or more applicants typically have longer waits. Your county operator can give you a realistic timeline when you apply.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep training if they find part-time work?
faqs.answer: No, participants must be unemployed throughout the training period. The program's goal is to transition to unsubsidized employment, so finding work means successfully completing the program.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent can't complete the full training period?
faqs.answer: Participants can leave the program at any time, and there are valid reasons for early exit including finding employment, health issues, or family circumstances. Early exit doesn't affect future eligibility if they become unemployed again.

faqs.question: Are there asset limits like other government programs?
faqs.answer: The sources don't mention asset limits, only income requirements. Contact your local SCSEP operator to confirm whether assets like savings accounts or property ownership affect eligibility.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for SCSEP and unemployment benefits at the same time?
faqs.answer: You'll need to check with both programs about overlapping benefits. SCSEP requires being unemployed, while unemployment benefits are for people actively seeking work, so there may be coordination requirements between the programs.

faqs.question: What if my parent has limited English or can't read well?
faqs.answer: People with limited English proficiency or low literacy actually get priority enrollment in SCSEP. The program is designed to help people with employment barriers, so language or literacy challenges make your parent more likely to be accepted.

programs.name: Senior Legal Helpline (via Area Agencies on Aging)
Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program (LTCOP)
programs.tagline: Free advocacy and complaint resolution if your parent lives in a nursing home or assisted living facility.

localEntities.name: Executive Office on Aging, 250 S. Hotel St., Ste. 406, Honolulu, Hawaii 96813-2831

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a nursing home, assisted living facility, or adult residential care home in Hawaii and you have concerns about their care, treatment, or rights, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy and complaint investigation. Certified ombudsmen investigate issues like inadequate care, medication errors, billing problems, or violations of your parent's rights as a resident.

The program is available to any resident or family member regardless of income or assets. Because ombudsmen are trained advocates who know facility regulations, they can often resolve complaints faster than families trying to navigate the system alone. They also provide confidential face-to-face visits and help families understand resident rights, payment options, and how to choose quality facilities.

otherRequirements: Must be a resident of (or acting on behalf of a resident in) a nursing home, assisted living facility, adult residential care home, expanded ARCH, CCFFH, hospice center, or adult day care in Hawaii

applicationGuide.tip: You don't need permission from your parent to file a complaint, but the ombudsman will respect confidentiality rules and may need consent before taking certain actions.

faqs.question: Can I file a complaint even if my parent doesn't want me to get involved?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can report concerns to the ombudsman, but they must follow confidentiality rules. The ombudsman may need your parent's consent before taking certain investigative actions or sharing specific information with you. They can still provide general guidance and may observe conditions during routine facility visits.

faqs.question: What kinds of problems can the ombudsman actually fix?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen can investigate and help resolve issues like inadequate care, medication errors, billing disputes, violations of resident rights, problems with personal belongings, and concerns about facility conditions. They work directly with facility staff and administrators to address problems and can escalate to regulatory agencies when necessary.

faqs.question: Does using the ombudsman program affect my parent's relationship with the facility?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen are trained to handle complaints professionally and facilities are prohibited from retaliating against residents or families who file complaints. The program focuses on resolving issues constructively rather than creating adversarial situations. However, ombudsmen can escalate to regulatory enforcement if facilities don't cooperate.

programs.name: Kahi Malama – A Place of Caring
programs.tagline: Free care coordination to help your Big Island parent find and navigate aging services.
programs.intro:
If your parent lives on Hawaii's Big Island and needs help navigating aging services, Kahi Malama provides free care coordination and options counseling. Because this is Hawaii County's official Aging & Disability Resource Center (ADRC), one care coordinator can help connect your parent to multiple programs — from meal delivery and respite care to Medicaid waivers and long-term care alternatives.

The program serves Hawaii County residents age 60 and older, plus adults with disabilities and their caregivers. There are no strict income limits, though services prioritize those with lower incomes. You can access help through offices in both Hilo and Kona, with island-wide service coordination.

otherRequirements: Must be Hawaii County (Big Island) resident
Focus on those needing aging in place support or living with disabilities

steps.title: Receive personalized action plan
steps.description: Your care coordinator will identify relevant programs and help you apply for services like meal delivery, transportation, or Medicaid waivers.

applicationGuide.tip: Have a list of your parent's current challenges ready — difficulty with bathing, medication management, meal preparation, or transportation — to help staff identify the best services.

faqs.question: Can I call on behalf of my parent who lives on the Big Island?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can initiate contact and participate in care planning. Staff will work with you to assess your parent's needs and identify appropriate services, though they may need to speak directly with your parent for some program applications.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the Hilo and Kona offices?
faqs.answer: Both offices provide the same aging and disability resource services island-wide. Choose based on convenience — Hilo serves (808) 961-8626, Kona serves (808) 323-4390 or 323-4392. Either office can help residents anywhere on the Big Island.

faqs.question: Does this program provide actual services or just referrals?
faqs.answer: Kahi Malama primarily provides care coordination, options counseling, and referrals to other programs. They'll help you navigate and apply for services like meal delivery, transportation, respite care, or Medicaid waivers, but don't directly provide those services themselves.

faqs.question: My parent lives on Maui — can they use this program?
faqs.answer: No, Kahi Malama only serves Hawaii County (Big Island) residents. Each Hawaiian island has its own Area Agency on Aging. Maui, Oahu, and Kauai residents need to contact their local aging offices for similar services.

faqs.question: Is there a waitlist for care coordination services?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify waitlist information for initial consultations. Call your regional office directly to ask about current availability. Care coordination is typically available fairly quickly, though specific programs they refer you to may have their own waitlists.

faqs.question: What if my parent doesn't meet the age requirement but has a disability?
faqs.answer: Adults of any age with disabilities can access services through Kahi Malama, along with their caregivers. You'll need documentation of the disability when you call for your initial assessment.

programs.name: Elderly Affairs Division (EAD) / Honolulu ADRC
programs.tagline: Get home care services, meal delivery, and caregiver support for your aging parent in Honolulu County.

localEntities.name: Honolulu County: Elderly Affairs Division, 222 North School Street, Honolulu, Hawaii 96817, (808) 768-7700

Maui County: Contact Executive Office on Aging for referral
Kauai County: Kauai Agency on Elderly Affairs, 4444 Rice Street, Suite 330, Lihue, Hawaii 96766, (808) 241-4470

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and needs help with daily activities in Honolulu County, the Elderly Affairs Division provides home care services, meal delivery, and caregiver support at no cost based on income. Services include personal care assistance (bathing, grooming), homemaker help (meal prep, shopping), delivered meals, home modifications like grab bars, and respite support for you as the caregiver.

To qualify, your parent must have cognitive impairment OR be unable to perform at least two daily activities (bathing, dressing, medication management, cooking, housework). They cannot be receiving similar services from other government programs or living in a nursing home or assisted living facility. Income and asset limits apply but are not published — you must call to learn if your parent qualifies financially.

This program serves Honolulu County only. If your parent lives on another Hawaiian island, contact their county's separate Area Agency on Aging for similar services.

faqs.question: Can my parent get services if they live in assisted living?
faqs.answer: No. Anyone residing in an assisted living facility, nursing home, adult residential care home, or similar institution cannot receive EAD services. The program is designed for people living independently in their own homes.

faqs.question: What if my parent already gets some help from a private caregiver?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot receive EAD services if they're already getting comparable home care from other government programs or private services. However, if their current help doesn't cover all needed services, call (808) 768-7700 to discuss what might be available.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can call (808) 768-7700 to start the application process for their parent. However, the in-home assessment will need to include your parent directly to evaluate their functional abilities and care needs.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives on the Big Island or Maui?
faqs.answer: Honolulu EAD only serves Honolulu County (Oahu). If your parent lives on other Hawaiian islands, contact their county's Area Agency on Aging: Hawaii County at (808) 961-8626, Maui County Executive Office on Aging, or Kauai County at (808) 241-4470.

programs.name: Kaua'i Agency on Elderly Affairs
programs.tagline: Free in-home care services and meals for Kaua'i seniors who need help with daily activities.

programs.intro:
If your parent lives on Kaua'i and needs help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, they may qualify for free in-home care services through the county's elderly affairs office. The agency provides two types of support: Title III services (completely free for anyone 60+) and Kupuna Care (sliding-scale cost based on income for those with documented functional limitations).

Because this program covers personal care, meal delivery, and care coordination under one umbrella, your parent won't need to navigate multiple agencies. Services include help with Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating) and Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (shopping, housework, medication management). Your parent must live in their own home — the program doesn't serve nursing home residents or anyone in institutional care.

faqs.question: Can I apply for both Title III and Kupuna Care services at the same time?
faqs.answer: No, these are separate programs with different eligibility requirements. Title III is for any senior 60+ regardless of functional status, while Kupuna Care requires documented cognitive impairment or inability to perform at least two daily activities. The agency will determine which program fits your parent's situation during the assessment.

faqs.question: What if my parent already gets some help from Medicaid or VA benefits?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot receive services that duplicate existing coverage from government programs or private insurance. However, gaps in coverage may qualify for supplemental services. The case worker will review all current benefits during the phone assessment to avoid conflicts.

faqs.question: Can a family member call if my parent has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent cannot call due to cognitive limitations, a Power of Attorney or primary caregiver can initiate the application by calling (808) 241-4470. Have your legal documents ready to verify your authority to act on their behalf.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent lives in other Hawaiian counties?
faqs.answer: This program only serves Kaua'i County residents. Each Hawaiian county has its own Area Agency on Aging with separate contact information and local administration. Contact your parent's county office directly for services.

programs.name: Home and Community Services (Statewide via EOA)
programs.tagline: Keep your parent at home with Medicaid-funded care services instead of nursing home placement.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Hawaii's Home and Community Services program can provide care at home instead of requiring nursing home placement. Because this program covers assistance with daily activities, adult day care, and even community care foster family homes through Hawaii's Medicaid system (Med-QUEST), your parent can receive the care they need while staying in familiar surroundings.

This isn't automatic for seniors — your parent must qualify for Hawaii Medicaid and meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements (meaning they need the same level of daily assistance that would otherwise require nursing home care). Income over $1,530/month for a single person living at home must go toward care costs, and countable assets must stay below $2,000 for individuals or $3,000 for couples.

The program operates statewide through Med-QUEST's managed care system, but enrollment caps can create waiting lists for services. Services include in-home assistance, adult day care, and supervised community living options, but exclude certain facilities like Expanded Adult Residential Care Homes.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary residence is typically exempt from the $2,000 asset limit in Medicaid programs, though specific home equity caps may apply. The program is specifically designed to help people stay at home rather than enter nursing facilities.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is $1,600/month — can they still get services?
faqs.answer: Yes, but they'll need to pay $70/month toward their care costs (the amount over the $1,530 limit). They can still receive all covered services through the program.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for home care services?
faqs.answer: Waitlist times aren't specified in available information and likely vary by island and managed care organization. Contact Med-QUEST directly to ask about current availability in your area.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: You can assist with the application process, but you may need legal documentation like power of attorney to act on their behalf. Contact the Med-QUEST program to clarify what authorization they require for family members to apply.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this program and paying for private home care?
faqs.answer: This program is funded through Medicaid and provides services at no cost to eligible participants (beyond any required income contribution). Private care can cost $4,000-8,000+ monthly, while this covers the same services through Hawaii's managed care system.

faqs.question: Can my parent get both this program and regular Medicare benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, this program works alongside Medicare. Your parent keeps their Medicare coverage for medical services, while this program specifically covers long-term care assistance that Medicare doesn't provide.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's condition improves and they no longer need nursing home level care?
faqs.answer: They would no longer qualify for this specific program, but they should maintain their Med-QUEST Medicaid coverage for medical services. The managed care organization will reassess functional needs periodically to determine ongoing eligibility.

faqs.question: Does this program cover assisted living facilities?
faqs.answer: Yes, it covers some assisted living residences and community care foster family homes, but specifically excludes Expanded Adult Residential Care Homes, which are private pay only. Ask the managed care organization about approved facility options.

If your parent is 60+ and lives alone on Oahu with mobility or memory challenges, they may qualify for free transportation and companionship to essential appointments. Transportation Friends for Kupuna pairs your parent with a trained volunteer who provides rides to doctor visits, grocery stores, the post office, and other necessary destinations.

Because volunteers are age 55+ themselves, many clients find genuine friendship alongside practical help. The program enforces gender matching (female volunteers with female clients, male with male) and requires 1-2 weeks advance notice for appointments. Your parent must live independently in their own home and have minimal family or community support available.

faqs.question: Can my parent get same-day rides for urgent appointments?
faqs.answer: No, this program requires 1-2 weeks advance notice and depends on volunteer availability. This is not an emergency transportation service. For urgent medical needs, your parent should use ambulance services or Handi-Van if they qualify.

faqs.question: Will my parent always get the same volunteer driver?
faqs.answer: The program aims to create consistent pairings when possible, but volunteer availability varies. Gender matching is strictly enforced — female volunteers only drive female clients, and male volunteers only drive male clients.

faqs.question: What if my parent has some family support but still struggles with transportation?
faqs.answer: The program prioritizes seniors with minimal support, but call to discuss your parent's specific situation. Program staff evaluate each case individually and may still accept clients who have some family help but face transportation barriers.

programs.name: Senior Companion Program
programs.tagline: Trained volunteer companions provide up to 20 hours weekly of in-home assistance for frail elders needing help to stay independent.

localEntities.name: Partners with nonprofit agencies serving kupuna

programs.intro:
If your parent needs companionship and light assistance to remain safely at home, Hawaii's Senior Companion Program matches them with trained volunteer seniors who provide up to 20 hours per week of support. Companions help with grocery shopping, accompany your parent to medical appointments, prepare simple meals, and provide the social connection that prevents isolation.

The program serves frail elders (60+) who have been assessed as needing person-to-person assistance to maintain independent living or who are scheduled for release from a healthcare facility within 2-4 weeks and need support to return home safely. Because companions are trained volunteers rather than paid caregivers, there's no cost to families — though services are subject to funding availability and volunteer recruitment.

This is particularly valuable for families who live far from an aging parent or need respite from daily caregiving duties. The program operates statewide through Hawaii's Department of Human Services, with the main office on Oahu and partnerships with nonprofit agencies serving kupuna across the islands.

faqs.answer: The program has two groups: volunteer companions (low-income seniors 55-60+ who provide the services) and clients (frail elders 60+ who receive the services). faqs.question: Can my parent get a companion if they live on the Big Island or Maui?
faqs.answer: The program is statewide, but the main office is on Oahu at (808) 832-0340. They work with nonprofit partners on other islands, but volunteer availability may be more limited. Call the main office to ask about services in your parent's specific area.

faqs.question: What if my parent's needs are too complex for a companion volunteer?
faqs.answer: Companions provide social support and light assistance, not medical care or full attendant services. If your parent needs help with bathing, medication management, or other clinical care, ask program staff about referrals to Kupuna Care or other home-based services that provide more intensive support.

faqs.question: How long will my parent have to wait for a companion?
faqs.answer: Wait times depend on volunteer availability in your area and program funding. The program relies on recruiting senior volunteers, so availability can fluctuate. Some areas may have immediate matches while others have waiting periods — program staff can give you a realistic timeline for your location.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my parent who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you're the legal guardian, you can sign the care plan on your parent's behalf. The program requires either the individual or their legal guardian to authorize visits and specify activities. You'll need to provide documentation of your guardianship status.

faqs.question: What happens if the companion and my parent don't get along?
faqs.answer: Program staff supervise the volunteer matches and can reassign a different companion if personalities don't mesh. Because companions are trained volunteers rather than employees, the program has flexibility to make changes when needed to ensure a good fit.

programs.name: Foster Grandparent Program
programs.tagline: Paid volunteer work helping special needs children if your parent is 55+ and on a low income.

localEntities.name: Hawaii Island: State of Hawaii Dept. of Human Services (contact via main office)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and has limited income, they can earn a non-taxable stipend working 15-20 hours per week as a Foster Grandparent, providing one-on-one support to children with special needs in schools and childcare centers. The program requires commitment to serving children (not adult caregiving) and your parent must be physically and mentally able to work with children who have developmental, learning, or behavioral challenges.

This is a structured volunteer position with employment benefits including meal allowances, travel reimbursement, paid vacation and sick leave. Your parent must have annual income around 125% of the federal poverty level (exact amounts vary by household size and change yearly), pass a physical exam, and complete a background check. The program serves children up to age 21 and requires a minimum 15-hour weekly commitment.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they're already receiving Social Security or have a small pension?
faqs.answer: Yes, but total annual income from all sources must stay at or below program guidelines (typically around 125% of federal poverty level). Social Security and pension income count toward this limit. Contact (808) 832-5169 for current income thresholds for your household size.

faqs.question: What if my parent has never worked with special needs children before?
faqs.answer: No prior experience required, but your parent must be willing and able to work with children who have developmental, learning, or behavioral challenges. The program provides required training after acceptance. The key requirement is genuine desire to help children and ability to handle the physical and emotional demands.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose which school or childcare center to work at?
faqs.answer: Placement depends on program needs and available positions. Your parent will work where Foster Grandparents are most needed, typically in schools, Head Start programs, or childcare centers serving children with special needs. Location preferences can be discussed but aren't guaranteed.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income changes after they're accepted?
faqs.answer: Income is reviewed periodically. If income exceeds guidelines by more than 20%, your parent may lose eligibility. Report income changes to the program office promptly. Temporary increases might be acceptable, but sustained income above limits ends participation.

faqs.question: Does my parent need their own transportation to work sites?
faqs.answer: Your parent needs reliable transportation to assigned locations. The program provides travel reimbursement, but doesn't provide transportation itself. Public transit, personal vehicle, or family transportation arrangements all work as long as your parent can consistently reach their assigned site.

faqs.question: How long is the wait to get placed after being accepted?
faqs.answer: Timing depends on funding availability and open positions at participating sites. The program operates subject to federal funding levels, so there may be waiting periods. Contact (808) 832-5169 for current availability and expected wait times.

programs.name: Memory Mentor Program
programs.tagline: Free in-home visits, safety checks, and daily support for seniors with memory loss living alone.
programs.intro:
If your parent has memory loss and lives alone with minimal caregiving support, they may qualify for free volunteer visits through Catholic Charities Hawaiʻi. Volunteers provide friendly visits, safety checks, grocery shopping, help reviewing mail, and medication reminders — all at no cost.

The program focuses on seniors who are isolated and need basic daily support but don't qualify for or require comprehensive medical care programs. Because it's volunteer-driven, availability depends on volunteer capacity in your area.

otherRequirements: Must be a senior with memory loss
Living alone with little or no caregiving support

faqs.question: What if there are no volunteers available in my parent's area?
faqs.answer: Because this is a volunteer-based program, availability depends on volunteer capacity in your specific location on Oʻahu. Call (808) 527-4777 to check current availability and ask about being placed on a waiting list if needed.

faqs.question: Can my parent receive these services if they already have some family help?
faqs.answer: The program is designed for seniors with "little or no caregiving support," but Catholic Charities evaluates each situation individually. If your parent needs additional support beyond what family can provide, they may still qualify.

stateOverview.intro:
Hawaii offers 19 senior programs that can save your aging parent hundreds to thousands of dollars monthly while keeping them safely at home. These range from Medicaid coverage (Med-QUEST) that pays for all health care including nursing home costs, to the Kūpuna Care Program that provides daily care help when Medicaid isn't available, to smaller supports like free meal delivery and transportation.

Many programs have income limits around $2,000-3,000 monthly for a single person, but several free resources — like Medicare counseling through Hawaii SHIP and advocacy through the Long-Term Care Ombudsman — are available regardless of income. Start with the health and care programs below, as these typically offer the highest dollar value and can prevent costly emergency situations.

startHere.name: Med-QUEST
startHere.why: Covers all health care costs including nursing home care for seniors with limited income — potentially worth thousands monthly.

startHere.name: Kūpuna Care Program
startHere.why: Provides attendant care, meals, and transportation for seniors 60+ who need daily help but don't qualify for Medicaid.

startHere.name: SNAP
startHere.why: Monthly grocery assistance that Hawaii residents may qualify for even with higher incomes than other states.

startHere.name: Hawaii SHIP
startHere.why: Free expert Medicare counseling helps find cost-saving programs and avoid expensive coverage mistakes.

byNeed.description: Free health coverage through Medicaid or expert help navigating Medicare to reduce costs.

byNeed.need: Daily care and staying at home
byNeed.description: Attendant care, companionship, and support services to help your parent remain independent at home.
Monthly grocery assistance and free meal delivery for seniors who can't shop or cook safely.
Help with electric and gas bills plus free home energy improvements to reduce ongoing costs.
Free respite care, training, and specialized support for caregivers of seniors with memory loss.
Free rides to appointments and transportation assistance for seniors with limited mobility.

quickFacts: Hawaii has county-specific programs: Honolulu uses Elderly Affairs Division, Kaua'i has its own Agency on Elderly Affairs, and Big Island residents can access Kahi Malama services.
The state's Kūpuna Care Program fills a gap for seniors who need daily care help but don't qualify for Medicaid.
Hawaii SNAP recipients may qualify with higher incomes than other states due to the state's cost of living adjustments.
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Hawaii offers both income-qualified benefits and free resources for all seniors. Benefits like Med-QUEST, Kūpuna Care, and SNAP require meeting income limits (typically under $2,000-3,000 monthly), but free resources like Hawaii SHIP Medicare counseling, Long-Term Care Ombudsman advocacy, and Memory Mentor Program support are available to any Hawaii senior regardless of income or assets.
